skill.name: ckm-design-system
skill.description: Build token-based design systems with primitive, semantic, and component token layers. Includes component specifications, Tailwind mapping guidance, slide-deck decision data, and local utilities for token generation and validation.
skill.summary: Create design tokens, component specs, Tailwind mappings, and brand-compliant slide systems.
skill.author: nextlevelbuilder

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity matches, but most weak-cryptography, C2, reconnaissance, and backtick alerts are false positives from CSS tokens, markdown examples, and slide CSV vocabulary. Confirmed concerns are legitimate developer-tool risks: local scripts read and write user-supplied paths, a wrapper delegates to another Python validator, and generated slides can load third-party assets.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Path Reads and Writes in Utility Scripts
medium_findings.description: Token and slide generators resolve CLI paths from the current working directory and write generated CSS or HTML to output paths. This is expected developer tooling, but users should run it only in trusted repositories to avoid accidental overwrite or broad local file reads.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads CLI-provided config or directory paths and writes to CLI-provided output paths. The behavior is legitimate for local design tooling, but the filesystem impact is clear.

medium_findings.title: Subprocess Delegation in Legacy Validator Wrapper
medium_findings.description: The legacy slide validator builds a command with the current Python executable and a fixed local validator, then appends user arguments. It does not use shell execution, so command injection risk is limited, but it still executes another local script in the active environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is confirmed and user arguments are forwarded. Risk is moderated because the command is an argument list and does not set shell=True.

medium_findings.title: Generated Slides Reference Third-Party Assets
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Chart.js CDN use, includes Google Fonts links in generated slide HTML, and provides curated Pexels image URLs. These dependencies are normal for presentations but can expose users to third-party availability, tracking, and supply-chain considerations when opened in a browser.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: External asset references are explicit and easy to verify. I did not find evidence of data exfiltration, so this is a dependency and privacy warning rather than a malicious-network finding.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and C2 Scanner Matches Are Design Content
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and C2 alerts map to CSS hex colors, token names, markdown examples, copywriting terms, and CSV slide vocabulary. No cryptographic API, command-and-control endpoint, or encoded payload was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain CSS color values, design-token references, and copywriting formulas. They do not contain crypto code or malicious network control logic.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are Documentation Fences
low_findings.description: Ruby or shell backtick detections in documentation are markdown code fences and inline file references, not Ruby execution. The examples describe local CLI usage for token generation, validation, and slide search.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation fences, diagrams, and command examples. They are not executable Ruby code paths.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Alerts Are Configuration Field Reads
low_findings.description: The env_access alerts correspond to reading slide configuration fields such as search keywords, image category, and overlay style. No os.environ or process.env secret access was found in the reviewed locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code reads dictionary fields from CSV-derived configuration objects. I did not find environment variable access at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Writes to CLI Output Path
dangerous_patterns.description: Generation utilities create parent directories and write output files based on command-line options. This is useful but should be run with explicit output paths in trusted workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are direct and confirmed. The risk is bounded because these are local CLI tools and there is no evidence of covert file targeting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Wrapper Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: A compatibility wrapper invokes another Python validator process. It uses a fixed script path and argument array, which reduces injection risk but remains an external command pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is real, but the lack of shell invocation and the fixed target validator reduce severity.

content.value_statement: Design systems often drift because tokens, components, and slide assets are documented separately. This skill provides a structured token model, component specs, validation helpers, and presentation guidance in one workflow.
actual_capabilities: Defines a three-layer token architecture using primitive, semantic, and component tokens.
Provides component specifications for buttons, inputs, cards, states, variants, and spacing.
Maps token concepts into Tailwind CSS configuration and utility patterns.
Includes starter token JSON for color, spacing, radius, shadow, and component values.
Provides local scripts for token generation, token validation, slide search, and HTML slide validation.
Includes CSV decision data for slide layouts, copy formulas, charts, typography, colors, and backgrounds.
limitations: The helper scripts are local CLI tools and should be run only in trusted workspaces.
Generated slides may reference third-party assets such as fonts, Chart.js, or Pexels images.
The skill provides guidance and templates, not a complete production design-system package.
Token validation checks common hardcoded values but cannot prove full visual accessibility compliance.

use_cases.title: Create a New Token System
use_cases.description: Convert brand colors, spacing, and typography into primitive, semantic, and component token layers.

use_cases.title: Prepare Design-to-Code Specs
use_cases.description: Use component specs and Tailwind mapping guidance to implement consistent buttons, inputs, cards, and states.

use_cases.title: Build Brand-Compliant Presentations
use_cases.description: Select slide layouts, chart patterns, copy formulas, colors, and backgrounds for persuasive presentation decks.

prompt_templates.title: Start Token Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a three-layer design token architecture for my product. Use primitive, semantic, and component layers, and explain the purpose of each layer.

prompt_templates.title: Specify Core Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Define token-based specifications for buttons, inputs, and cards. Include variants, states, spacing, typography, and implementation notes.

prompt_templates.title: Map Tokens to Tailwind
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this design-token structure into a Tailwind theme plan. Include color, spacing, radius, typography, and dark-mode guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Token-Compliant Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a brand-compliant presentation deck for this audience and goal. Recommend slide strategy, layouts, typography, color treatment, charts, copy formulas, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A startup needs a design-token foundation for a new SaaS interface.
output_examples.output: A layered token plan with base colors and spacing, semantic aliases for product meaning, and component tokens for buttons, inputs, cards, and states.

output_examples.input: A frontend team wants Tailwind classes to match design-system tokens.
output_examples.output: A Tailwind integration outline that maps token names to theme values, documents dark-mode behavior, and identifies where raw values should be replaced.

output_examples.input: A sales team needs a persuasive investor deck that follows brand rules.
output_examples.output: A slide plan with narrative structure, layout recommendations, chart choices, copy formulas, background guidance, and token-compliance checks.

best_practices: Start with primitive tokens, then create semantic aliases before defining component-specific tokens.
Run validation tools in a trusted workspace and review all generated files before committing them.
Use slide decision data as guidance, then adapt layouts and copy to the real audience and brand voice.
anti_patterns: Do not put raw hex colors, spacing values, or font names directly into component styles.
Do not run the local scripts against sensitive directories or broad filesystem roots.
Do not treat generated presentation content as final without checking accessibility, licensing, and factual accuracy.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps create token-based design systems, component specs, Tailwind mappings, and brand-compliant presentation decks.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready UI code?
faq.answer: It provides patterns, tokens, specs, and utilities. Teams still need to integrate and test the final UI code.

faq.question: Can it work with Tailwind CSS?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Tailwind integration guidance for colors, spacing, radius, typography, states, and dark mode.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Most guidance is local. Generated slides may reference Google Fonts, Chart.js, or Pexels URLs if those patterns are used.

faq.question: Are the bundled scripts safe to run?
faq.answer: They appear intended for local developer workflows. Run them only in trusted repositories and review output paths before execution.

faq.question: Can it validate every design-system issue?
faq.answer: No. The validators check common token violations, but teams still need accessibility, browser, and brand-quality reviews.
